Home › Electroweak Baryogenesis

Electroweak Baryogenesis

Electroweak Baryogenesis (EWBG) is a theoretical mechanism that seeks to explain the Matter-Antimatter Asymmetry of the universe using the physics of the Electroweak phase transition. Unlike models that rely on the distant GUT Scale, EWBG occurs at a much lower energy (\( \sim 100 ext{ GeV} \)), making it potentially testable at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC). It relies on the rapid movement of Higgs field "bubbles" to create a non-equilibrium environment where Sphalerons can forge matter from leptons.

1. The Electroweak Phase Transition

When the universe was \( 10^{-11} \) seconds old, it cooled enough for the Higgs field to acquire a non-zero value, giving mass to particles. For EWBG to work, this transition must be Strongly First-Order. This means the universe does not change smoothly; instead, bubbles of the "New Vacuum" (where particles have mass) nucleate and expand into the "Old Vacuum" (where they are massless), creating the Departure from Equilibrium required by Sakharov.

2. Interaction with the Bubble Wall

As the bubble walls sweep through the primordial plasma, quarks and leptons reflect off or pass through them. Because the Standard Model contains CP Violation (via the CKM matrix), the reflection and transmission probabilities are slightly different for particles and antiparticles. This creates a "Chiral Charge" density in front of the wall, where a small surplus of left-handed fermions is generated.

3. Sphalerons and the Conversion

In the region of high temperature in front of the bubble wall, Sphalerons are extremely active. These non-perturbative configurations "sense" the chiral surplus and, through an Anomaly, convert the left-handed leptons into baryons. As the bubble expands, these newly created baryons are "swallowed" into the bubble interior. Inside the bubble, the Higgs field is high, the sphalerons are suppressed, and the Baryon Number is safely frozen in.

4. The Higgs Boson Mass Problem

A major challenge for EWBG within the Standard Model is that the Higgs boson discovered at the LHC is too heavy (\( 125 ext{ GeV} \)). A heavy Higgs leads to a "Crossover" transition rather than a strong first-order one. For EWBG to remain viable, "New Physics" such as additional Higgs bosons or Supersymmetry must be added to "stiffen" the transition and provide the necessary bubble dynamics.

5. Search for New CP Violation

In addition to the bubble problem, the Standard Model's CKM phase is too weak to generate the observed Baryon-to-Photon Ratio. EWBG requires a new, much stronger source of CP violation. Physicists look for this by measuring the Electric Dipole Moments of electrons and neutrons; a detection of a non-zero EDM would be the "Smoking Gun" that the electroweak era had the CP-violating power required to build our world.

6. Probing the Era with Gravitational Waves

If the electroweak phase transition was indeed strongly first-order, the collision of vacuum bubbles would have produced a stochastic background of Gravitational Waves. These waves would have a characteristic frequency reachable by future detectors like LISA. Observing this signal would allow us to "hear" the moment when the Higgs field turned on and the matter-antimatter asymmetry was finalized, linking particle physics to the Total Dynamics of the cosmos.

Key Theoretical Identities

Interact with identities to explore their semantic depth.

  • Phase Boundary [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ \phi(T) eq 0 \implies ext{Symmetry Breaking} \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Phase Boundary: The expanding front where the Higgs field transitions, creating a non-equilibrium zone.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.

  • Handedness Bias [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ v(T_c) / T_c \gtrsim 1 \quad ( ext{Strongly First Order}) \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Handedness Bias: The temporary excess of left-handed particles created by CP-violating wall interactions.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.

  • Internal Protection [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ \Gamma_{sph} \propto e^{-E_{sph}/T} \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Internal Protection: How the bubble interior shields new baryons from being destroyed by inverse reactions.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.

  • Discovery Path [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ n_B \sim \frac{\Gamma_{sph}}{H} \Delta_{CP} \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Discovery Path: Why the known Higgs mass requires BSM particles to explain our existence.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.
